article_title: Amazon S3 Turns 20 Storing Over 500 Trillion Objects Across Hundreds of Exabytes as AWS Rewrites Core Components in Rust
summary: Summary field contains text from an unrelated article (SMILE spacecraft). Article body and sources are excellent but the summary must be corrected.

content_preview.title: Amazon S3 Turns 20 Storing Over 500 Trillion Objects Across Hundreds of Exabytes as AWS Rewrites Core Components in Rust
content_preview.summary: The joint ESA-Chinese Academy of Sciences SMILE spacecraft reached French Guiana after a two-week ocean voyage and is preparing for an April launch on a Vega-C rocket. It will be the first mission to image Earth's magnetosphere in soft X-rays.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
Amazon Web Services celebrated the 20th anniversary of Amazon Simple Storage Service on March 14, a date chosen for the original 2006 launch because it falls on Pi Day. What began as a service running on roughly 400 storage nodes across 15 racks in three data centers with approximately one petabyte of total capacity and 15 Gbps of bandwidth has grown into what AWS describes as the backbone of the modern internet.

S3 now [stores more than 500 trillion objects, serves over 200 million requests pe...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent article body. Well-structured News piece covering S3's 20th anniversary with clear sections on scale, API stability, Rust rewrites, data lake evolution, and security history. Strong writing quality.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 sources fetched. The Register confirms all major stats (500T+ objects, 200M+ req/s, hundreds of exabytes, 123 AZs, 39 regions, original scale, Stormacq quote, Rust rewrites, 11-nines durability, public access defaults). SiliconANGLE confirms Andy Warfield's role, million+ data lakes, AI workload infrastructure, Hadoop S3A connector. AWS blog page returned mostly CSS/metadata but the URL is a legitimate primary source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims in the body verified against sources. However, the SUMMARY FIELD is completely wrong — it describes the ESA/CAS SMILE spacecraft mission, which belongs to a different article entirely. This is clearly cross-contamination from parallel agent execution.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or AI self-references.
editor_notes.originality: No existing S3 anniversary coverage in the archive.
concerns: CRITICAL: The summary field reads 'The joint ESA-Chinese Academy of Sciences SMILE spacecraft reached French Guiana...' — this has nothing to do with Amazon S3. The summary is from a completely different submission. This must be corrected before publication as it would appear in article metadata, RSS feeds, and social cards.
recommendations: Fix the summary to accurately describe the S3 anniversary article content
aws.amazon.com is a legitimate primary source and the allowlist warning can be ignored
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The article body is publication-ready — well-researched, properly sourced, and well-written. However, the wrong summary makes this submission unpublishable as-is. REQUEST_CHANGES to fix the summary field only.
